topics.title: Public Key
topics.definition: A Public Key is one half of an asymmetric key pair. It is freely distributed and used to encrypt data, verify digital signatures, or establish secure communication.
examPoints: A Public Key is one half of an asymmetric key pair. It is freely distributed and used to encrypt data, verify digital signatures, or establish secure communication.
topics.narration:
Public Key

A Public Key is one half of an asymmetric key pair. It is freely distributed and used to encrypt data, verify digital signatures, or establish secure communication.

Important exam points. A Public Key is one half of an asymmetric key pair. It is freely distributed and used to encrypt data, verify digital signatures, or establish secure communication.. Can be shared publicly.. Stored inside the X.509 Certificate.

topics.title: Private Key
topics.definition: A Private Key is the secret half of an asymmetric key pair. It is used to decrypt data, create digital signatures, and prove ownership of a certificate.
examPoints: A Private Key is the secret half of an asymmetric key pair. It is used to decrypt data, create digital signatures, and prove ownership of a certificate.
If compromised → Revoke certificate + Generate new key pair.
topics.narration:
Private Key

A Private Key is the secret half of an asymmetric key pair. It is used to decrypt data, create digital signatures, and prove ownership of a certificate.

Important exam points. A Private Key is the secret half of an asymmetric key pair. It is used to decrypt data, create digital signatures, and prove ownership of a certificate.. Never shared.. If compromised → Revoke certificate + Generate new key pair.

topics.title: Public Key vs Private Key
comparisons: Public Key: Shared; Private Key: Secret
Public Key: Encrypt / Verify; Private Key: Decrypt / Sign
Public Key: Stored in Certificate; Private Key: Stored Securely
topics.narration:
Public Key vs Private Key

Important exam points. Private = Protect

Comparison. Public Key: Shared; Private Key: Secret. Public Key: Encrypt / Verify; Private Key: Decrypt / Sign. Public Key: Stored in Certificate; Private Key: Stored Securely

topics.title: Key Pair
topics.definition: A key pair consists of a mathematically related Public Key and Private Key generated together.
examPoints: A key pair consists of a mathematically related Public Key and Private Key generated together.
Public key is derived from the private key.
One cannot feasibly derive the private key from the public key.
topics.narration:
Key Pair

A key pair consists of a mathematically related Public Key and Private Key generated together.

Important exam points. A key pair consists of a mathematically related Public Key and Private Key generated together.. Public key is derived from the private key.. One cannot feasibly derive the private key from the public key.

topics.title: CSR (Certificate Signing Request)
topics.definition: A CSR is a request sent to a Certificate Authority containing the applicant's public key and identity information for certificate issuance.
examPoints: A CSR is a request sent to a Certificate Authority containing the applicant's public key and identity information for certificate issuance.
topics.narration:
CSR (Certificate Signing Request)

A CSR is a request sent to a Certificate Authority containing the applicant's public key and identity information for certificate issuance.

Important exam points. A CSR is a request sent to a Certificate Authority containing the applicant's public key and identity information for certificate issuance.. Contains:. Public Key. Subject. SAN. Applicant Signature. Never contains:. Private Key

topics.title: CSR vs Certificate
comparisons: CSR: Request; Certificate: Final Identity
CSR: Signed by Applicant; Certificate: Signed by CA
CSR: No Issuer; Certificate: Has Issuer
CSR: No Validity; Certificate: Has Validity
topics.narration:
CSR vs Certificate

Comparison. CSR: Request; Certificate: Final Identity. CSR: Signed by Applicant; Certificate: Signed by CA. CSR: No Issuer; Certificate: Has Issuer. CSR: No Validity; Certificate: Has Validity

topics.title: PEM
topics.definition: PEM (Privacy Enhanced Mail) is a Base64-encoded text format used to store certificates, keys, and CSRs.
examPoints: PEM (Privacy Enhanced Mail) is a Base64-encoded text format used to store certificates, keys, and CSRs.
topics.narration:
PEM

PEM (Privacy Enhanced Mail) is a Base64-encoded text format used to store certificates, keys, and CSRs.

Important exam points. PEM (Privacy Enhanced Mail) is a Base64-encoded text format used to store certificates, keys, and CSRs.. Begins with. Commonly used in:. Apache. Nginx. OpenSSL. Linux

Workflow. -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----

topics.title: DER
topics.definition: DER (Distinguished Encoding Rules) is the binary encoding format of X.509 certificates and cryptographic objects.
examPoints: DER (Distinguished Encoding Rules) is the binary encoding format of X.509 certificates and cryptographic objects.
topics.narration:
DER

DER (Distinguished Encoding Rules) is the binary encoding format of X.509 certificates and cryptographic objects.

Important exam points. DER (Distinguished Encoding Rules) is the binary encoding format of X.509 certificates and cryptographic objects.. Binary. Smaller than PEM. Machine-readable

comparisons: PEM: Base64 Text; DER: Binary
PEM: Human Readable; DER: Machine Format
PEM: Linux/OpenSSL; DER: Windows/Java

PEM vs DER

topics.title: Trust Store
topics.definition: A Trust Store contains trusted Root CA and Intermediate CA certificates used to verify other certificates.

Trust Store

A Trust Store contains trusted Root CA and Intermediate CA certificates used to verify other certificates.

Important exam points. A Trust Store contains trusted Root CA and Intermediate CA certificates used to verify other certificates.. Contains. Certificates only. Never Private Keys

topics.title: Key Store
topics.definition: A Key Store stores the owner's private key along with the associated certificate and certificate chain.
examPoints: A Key Store stores the owner's private key along with the associated certificate and certificate chain.

Key Store

topics.title: Certificate Chain File
topics.definition: A certificate chain file contains the server certificate followed by Intermediate CA certificates.
examPoints: A certificate chain file contains the server certificate followed by Intermediate CA certificates.

Certificate Chain File

A certificate chain file contains the server certificate followed by Intermediate CA certificates.

Important exam points. A certificate chain file contains the server certificate followed by Intermediate CA certificates.

Workflow. Server ↓ Intermediate ↓ Root (Usually not included)

topics.title: Full Chain
topics.definition: A Full Chain contains the server certificate and all Intermediate CA certificates needed for validation.
examPoints: A Full Chain contains the server certificate and all Intermediate CA certificates needed for validation.
Usually does not include Root CA because clients already trust it.

Full Chain

A Full Chain contains the server certificate and all Intermediate CA certificates needed for validation.

topics.title: Root Certificate
topics.definition: The self-signed certificate belonging to the Root Certificate Authority.

topics.title: Intermediate Certificate
topics.definition: A certificate issued by the Root CA used to issue end-entity certificates.

Most certificates are signed by Intermediate CA

Intermediate Certificate

A certificate issued by the Root CA used to issue end-entity certificates.

topics.title: Create PKCS#12
commands: Exam Points: openssl pkcs12 \ -export \ -out server.pfx \ -inkey server.key \ -in server.crt

Create PKCS#12

Important exam points. PFX / P12

Commands. Exam Points: openssl pkcs12 \ -export \ -out server.pfx \ -inkey server.key \ -in server.crt

topics.title: Verify Certificate Matches Private Key
commands: Compare public key hashes: openssl x509 -pubkey -noout -in server.crt
topics.narration:
Verify Certificate Matches Private Key

Commands. Compare public key hashes: openssl x509 -pubkey -noout -in server.crt. and: openssl pkey -pubout -in server.key

Extension: .crt; Contains: Certificate; Private Key: No
Extension: .cer; Contains: Certificate; Private Key: No
Extension: .key; Contains: Private Key; Private Key: Yes
Extension: .csr; Contains: Certificate Request; Private Key: No
Extension: .pfx; Contains: Certificate + Key + Chain; Private Key: Yes
Extension: .p12; Contains: Certificate + Key + Chain; Private Key: Yes
Extension: .jks; Contains: Java KeyStore; Private Key: Yes

Extension: .csr; Contains: Certificate Request; Private Key: No. Extension: .pfx; Contains: Certificate + Key + Chain; Private Key: Yes. Extension: .p12; Contains: Certificate + Key + Chain; Private Key: Yes. Extension: .jks; Contains: Java KeyStore; Private Key: Yes

topics.title: Top 30 One-Liners for Exam
oneLiners: Public Key is shared; Private Key is secret.
Certificates contain public keys, not private keys.
CSR contains public key, never private key.
CSR is signed by the applicant.
Certificate is signed by the CA.
PEM is Base64 text.
DER is binary.
CRT and CER are certificate file extensions.
KEY files usually contain private keys.
PFX and P12 are PKCS#12 containers.
PFX contains certificate + private key + chain.
JKS is Java's keystore format.
Trust Store contains trusted CA certificates only.
Trust Store never contains private keys.
Key Store contains your private key and certificate.
Root CA certificates are installed in trust stores.
Intermediate CAs issue end-entity certificates.
Full Chain = Server + Intermediate certificates.
The Root CA is usually not sent by the server during TLS.
File extensions do not determine the encoding; the content does.
PEM can store certificates, keys, CSRs, and chains.
DER is the binary representation of the same data.
openssl req creates a CSR.
openssl x509 displays certificate details.
openssl pkey displays private/public key details.
openssl pkcs12 manages PFX/P12 files.
Protect .key files with restrictive permissions.
Never email or publicly share a private key.
Backup private keys securely (preferably in an HSM or encrypted storage).
The most frequently tested formats are: PEM, DER, CRT, CER, KEY, PFX/PKCS#12, JKS, Trust Store, and Key Store.

PFX contains certificate + private key + chain.. JKS is Java's keystore format.. Trust Store contains trusted CA certificates only.. Trust Store never contains private keys.. Key Store contains your private key and certificate.. Root CA certificates are installed in trust stores.. Intermediate CAs issue end-entity certificates.. Full Chain = Server + Intermediate certificates.. The Root CA is usually not sent by the server during TLS.. File extensions do not determine the encoding; the content does.. PEM can store certificates, keys, CSRs, and chains.. DER is the binary representation of the same data.. openssl req creates a CSR.. openssl x509 displays certificate details.. openssl pkey displays private/public key details.. openssl pkcs12 manages PFX/P12 files.. Protect .key files with restrictive permissions.. Never email or publicly share a private key.. Backup private keys securely (preferably in an HSM or encrypted storage).. The most frequently tested formats are: PEM, DER, CRT, CER, KEY, PFX/PKCS#12, JKS, Trust Store, and Key Store.
